problem_statement: Dashboard.tsx is an oversized monolithic component combining data fetching, layout, multiple widget renderings, and state management in a single file. Its size makes it hard to understand, test, and modify without risking unintended side effects. Decomposition into focused modules isolates concerns and makes each piece independently testable.
in_scope: Analyse Dashboard.tsx to identify distinct responsibilities and logical boundaries
Split Dashboard.tsx into focused child components and/or utility modules
Use 200 LOC as a preferred target for resulting modules but do not force artificial splits where readability would worsen
Ensure the decomposed components compose to produce identical behaviour to the original Dashboard.tsx
Apply the typed prop interfaces defined in P2-R2-06 to the new child components
out_of_scope: Adding new dashboard features, widgets, or capabilities — R2 is purely structural
Visual or design changes to the dashboard — styling remains as-is
Decomposing other components — each hotspot has its own task
Changing the data model or API endpoints used by Dashboard
requirements: Dashboard.tsx is decomposed into focused modules with clear single responsibilities
Resulting modules target approximately 200 LOC each unless a larger module preserves better readability
All child components have typed prop interfaces
The composed result produces identical behaviour and rendering to the pre-decomposition Dashboard.tsx
acceptance_criteria: Dashboard.tsx is decomposed into clearer modules with preserved behaviour
No new features, visual changes, or API changes are introduced
npm run check passes with no new type errors introduced
The dashboard functions identically to the pre-refactor version
dependencies: P2-R2-06 — typed prop interfaces for Dashboard and its children should be defined
P3-R2-08 — shared hooks (useStages, useSubStages) should be available if Dashboard uses them
P3-R2-09 — apiRequest wrapper should be available if Dashboard makes API calls
risks: Dashboard may have complex inter-widget state dependencies that complicate clean decomposition
implementation_notes: Look for natural visual/logical boundaries: summary cards, charts, task lists, filters. Extract each widget into its own component. Use shared hooks for data fetching. Keep the top-level Dashboard as a composition root that arranges child components.
